1	import { defineConfig } from '@playwright/test';
2	import path from 'node:path';
3	import { fileURLToPath } from 'node:url';
4	
5	const __dirname = path.dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url));
6	
7	// The default Playwright harness boots the Go DDx server against a temp copy
8	// of e2e/fixtures/ so the API endpoints exercised by TC-008 (and other
9	// backend-dependent specs) return real data without reading the developer's
10	// $HOME or the repository's live .ddx/ state.
11	
12	const CLI_PKG_DIR = path.resolve(__dirname, '../../..');
13	const FRONTEND_DIR = __dirname;
14	const FIXTURE_DIR = path.resolve(__dirname, 'e2e/fixtures');
15	const PORT = Number(process.env.DDX_E2E_PORT ?? 4174);
16	const BASE_URL = `https://127.0.0.1:${PORT}`;
17	
18	// Build the SvelteKit frontend (so cli/internal/server/embed.go has assets to
19	// embed), copy the fixture workspace into a fresh temp dir, build the ddx
20	// binary from the cli module, and exec it from the temp dir. The temp dir has
21	// no .git so FindProjectRoot falls back to it, and tsnet is disabled so the
22	// harness has no Tailscale dependency.
23	const bootCommand = [
24		`set -e`,
25		`(cd "${FRONTEND_DIR}" && bun run build >&2)`,
26		`TMP=$(mktemp -d -t ddx-e2e-XXXXXX)`,
27		`cp -R "${FIXTURE_DIR}/." "$TMP/"`,
28		`export XDG_DATA_HOME="$TMP/.xdg-data"`,
29		// Build deterministically so VCS metadata does not block fixture boot.
30		`(cd "${CLI_PKG_DIR}" && go build -buildvcs=false -o "$TMP/ddx" .)`,
31		`cd "$TMP"`,
32		`DDX_OPERATOR_PROMPT_ALLOWLIST=localhost exec ./ddx server --tsnet=false --addr=127.0.0.1 --port=${PORT}`
33	].join(' && ');
34	
35	export default defineConfig({
36		webServer: {
37			command: `bash -c '${bootCommand}'`,
38			url: `${BASE_URL}/api/health`,
39			ignoreHTTPSErrors: true,
40			reuseExistingServer: !process.env.CI,
41			timeout: 180_000,
42			stdout: 'pipe',
43			stderr: 'pipe'
44		},
45		use: {
46			baseURL: BASE_URL,
47			ignoreHTTPSErrors: true
48		},
49		testMatch: ['**/*.e2e.{ts,js}', 'e2e/**/*.spec.ts']
50	});

data.output:
1	---
2	ddx:
3	  id: FEAT-008
4	  depends_on:
5	    - helix.prd
6	    - FEAT-002
7	    - FEAT-014
8	---
9	# Feature: DDx Server Web UI
10	
11	**ID:** FEAT-008
12	**Status:** In Progress
13	**Priority:** P1
14	**Owner:** DDx Team
15	
16	## Overview
17	
18	The DDx server (`ddx-server`) serves a web UI for browsing artifacts, beads,
19	the artifact dependency graph, and DDx run activity. Artifacts include markdown
20	documents plus sidecar-identified media such as diagrams, wireframes, images,
21	and PDFs. The UI is a SvelteKit application built with Svelte 5, embedded into
22	the Go binary via `embed.FS`, and served alongside the MCP and HTTP API
23	endpoints from a single process.
24	
25	`ddx-server` runs as a per-user host daemon (one instance per machine, see
26	FEAT-002 and FEAT-020), so the same UI enumerates every project that the
27	host+user registry knows about and binds its navigation to one selected
28	project context at a time. The node-aware URL structure and combined
29	cross-project dashboards are specified in FEAT-021; FEAT-008 owns the
30	underlying views that those routes render.
31	
32	The UI consumes GraphQL operations defined in `cli/internal/server/graphql/schema.graphql`.
33	Every page lists its required query or subscription by name for traceability.
34	
35	## Problem Statement
36	
37	**Current situation:** DDx artifacts, beads, and the artifact graph are only accessible via CLI commands or MCP tool calls. There's no visual way to browse the artifact library, see the dependency graph, check bead status, regenerate generated artifacts, or review layer-aware run evidence.
38	
39	**Pain points:**
40	- CLI output for `ddx doc graph` is text-only — dependency graphs need visual rendering
41	- Bead status and dependency trees are hard to grok in terminal output
42	- DDx run activity has no browsable interface that preserves the `work` -> `try` -> `run` layers
43	- Non-markdown artifacts have no type-aware browser preview, even when the graph knows their identity
44	- Generated artifacts have no UI affordance for checking provenance or requesting regeneration
45	- New team members can't quickly understand what artifacts exist or how they relate
46	
47	**Desired outcome:** A web dashboard served from the same `ddx-server` binary that already handles MCP and HTTP API. Developers open a browser, see their artifact library, dependency graph, bead board, generated-artifact provenance, and DDx run activity — all from one URL.
48	
49	## Architecture
50	
51	### Single Binary, Multiple Surfaces
52	
53	```
54	ddx-server binary
55	├── /            → Web UI (embedded SvelteKit)
56	├── /api/        → HTTP REST API (JSON) — frozen
57	└── /graphql     → GraphQL endpoint (gqlgen)
58	```
59	
60	The SvelteKit frontend queries the `/graphql` endpoint for all data. REST endpoints
61	remain unchanged and are consumed by the CLI and MCP tools only.
62	
63	### Project Context
64	
65	The UI is rendered under the node-scoped URL structure defined in FEAT-021:
66	`/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/...` for project-scoped views and
67	`/nodes/:nodeId/...` for combined node-wide dashboards. The root `/`
68	redirects to `/nodes/:nodeId` using the ID returned by `GET /api/node`
69	(FEAT-020). When more than one project is registered the navigation bar
70	exposes a project picker populated from `GET /api/projects`; selecting a
71	project swaps the `:projectId` segment in place. All GraphQL queries issued by the
72	UI are bound to the selected project context.
73	
74	### Project Navigation Tabs
75	
76	Each `/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/...` page exposes the following
77	project-scoped tabs in the global navigation, in this order:
78	
79	| Tab | Route segment | Purpose |
80	|---|---|---|
81	| Overview | (none) | Project home, recent activity, operator-prompt input (ADR-021) |
82	| Beads | `beads` | Bead list / kanban / ready / detail (US-082*) |
83	| Artifacts | `artifacts` | Artifact browser (US-080, US-081b, US-083) |
84	| Graph | `graph` | Document dependency graph (US-081, US-081c) |
85	| Runs | `runs` | Layer-aware run history with `work` / `try` / `run` chips, row expansion into the shared `work` / `try` / `run` run-detail tab strip, and re-queue (US-086b, US-086c) |
86	| Workers | `workers` | Live + recent worker processes for this project (US-085b, US-086, US-086a) |
87	| Personas | `personas` | Persona browser + role bindings (US-097) |
88	| Plugins | `plugins` | Plugin registry + install/uninstall (US-098) |
89	| Commits | `commits` | Project git commit log (US-093, FEAT-021) |
90	| Efficacy | `efficacy` | Model efficacy + comparisons (US-096) |
91	
92	**Sessions and Executions are retired as top-level activity tabs**
93	(Story 8). The unified Runs tab carries the shared layer-aware tab
94	structure and inline row expansion that surface what those tabs used to
95	show. The shared detail tabs are:
96	
97	- `work` rows: `overview`
98	- `try` rows: `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `tools`, `evidence`
99	- `run` rows: `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `session`, `tools`, `evidence`
100

1	---
2	ddx:
3	  id: FEAT-010
4	  depends_on:
5	    - helix.prd
6	    - FEAT-005
7	    - FEAT-006
8	---
9	# Feature: Task Execution
10	
11	**ID:** FEAT-010
12	**Status:** In Progress
13	**Priority:** P1
14	**Owner:** DDx Team
15	
16	## Overview
17	
18	DDx owns the task execution lifecycle. The lifecycle is made of concrete layers
19	with stable public names: `run`, `try`, and `work`. Each higher layer composes
20	the layer beneath it. There are no other run kinds beyond those named layers.
21	
22	| Layer | CLI | Inputs | Outputs / side effects | Owns |
23	|---|---|---|---|---|
24	| 1 | `ddx run` | prompt + execution config | structured output, side effects, run metadata (tokens, model, duration, exit) | invocation atom; consumes upstream Fizeau execution contract |
25	| 2 | `ddx try <bead>` | bead id, base revision | new worktree state + run evidence; merge or preserve | worktree start/end capture; bead → prompt resolution; side-effect bundling |
26	| 3 | `ddx work` | bead queue, stop conditions | sequence of `ddx try` records + loop-level record (drained / blocked / deferred) | mechanical queue drain; no-progress detection |
27	
28	`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`. `ddx work` iterates `ddx try`. One on-disk
29	substrate; layer metadata distinguishes records.
30	
31	Implementation code for this boundary should follow the task-execution
32	vocabulary (`taskexec` for core layer logic, `fizeauadapter` for the service
33	bridge). Treat `internal/agent` as legacy naming only; new DDx-owned execution
34	work should make the workflow boundary explicit.
35	
36	`ddx artifact regenerate <id>` is sugar over layer 1 (or layer 2 when the
37	generator edits the repo) with `produces_artifact: <id>` metadata. It is
38	not a fourth layer — it is a labeled invocation at an existing layer.
39	
40	## Agent Interaction Modes
41	
42	DDx defines four named agent interaction modes (`DDX_MODE`) to separate
43	broad interactive queue stewardship from worker execution. The mode governs
44	what an agent opened in a DDx project is permitted to do.
45	
46	| DDX_MODE | Description | Allowed actions | Prohibited actions |
47	|---|---|---|---|
48	| `queue_steward` | Default interactive mode. Surveys, triages, and advises on the queue without claiming or executing beads. | Read tracker and docs; report status; advise on bead quality; suggest readiness fixes; run `ddx bead list/ready/status/show`. | Claiming beads; creating isolated workspaces; starting `ddx work` or `ddx try` without an explicit user directive. |
49	| `bead_execution` | Worker mode. Executes one or more beads from the queue in isolated workspaces. | Full FEAT-010 layer-2 / layer-3 lifecycle: workspace creation, bead attempt, merge or preserve, evidence bundle. | Scope creep outside the named bead; switching to direct implementation without a bead. |
50	| `direct_user_implementation` | Human-directed implementation. The user has explicitly asked the agent to edit code or docs, bypassing the bead queue. | Edit code and docs as instructed; commit to the current branch. | Starting autonomous queue drain; claiming beads autonomously. |
51	| `review` | Read-only review. Grades an existing implementation against AC or specs. | Read code, docs, and evidence; return a structured verdict with per-AC findings. | Writing commits; claiming beads; modifying the tracker. |
52	
53	### Precedence
54	
55	`bead_execution` supersedes `queue_steward` when DDx invokes a worker
56	explicitly (via `ddx work`, `ddx try <id>`, or an internal execute-bead
57	worker invocation).
58	`direct_user_implementation` supersedes `queue_steward` when the user
59	explicitly asks for code or doc edits unrelated to the queue. `review`
60	supersedes `queue_steward` when the user explicitly requests a graded review
61	pass.
62	
63	Tracker instructions, merge-policy, and safety rules in AGENTS.md are
64	load-bearing in every mode and are never overridden by mode selection.
65	
66	### Lifecycle Migration Startup Gate
67	
68	`ddx work`, `ddx try`, server-managed workers, GraphQL worker starts, REST/MCP
69	worker starts, and queue status/readiness commands must run the TD-027 §18 lifecycle
70	startup preflight before claiming beads or deriving worker eligibility. When the
71	active queue still contains unmigrated legacy lifecycle labels or
72	pseudo-statuses, these surfaces fail closed with a configuration/startup error.
73	They do not call Fizeau, do not classify the failure as an agent attempt, and do
74	not add cooldown or retry metadata.
75	
76	Allowed bypass commands are limited to help/version output, `ddx doctor` or
77	other read-only diagnostics that do not mutate lifecycle, and the lifecycle
78	migration command:
79	
80	```bash
81	ddx bead migrate --lifecycle --dry-run
82	ddx bead migrate --lifecycle --apply
83	```
84	
85	The error must print counts of legacy labels and pseudo-statuses, a small sample
86	of affected bead IDs, and the exact migration command. The migration is one-way:
87	old `needs_human` and `triage:needs-investigation` lanes are not runtime
88	compatibility modes. If the migration is wrong, the recovery path is git
89	rollback of the tracker commit.
90	
91	### Worker-capacity suggestion rules
92	
93	An agent in `queue_steward` mode may suggest running `ddx work` or point
94	the operator to worker-capacity settings when:
95	
96	- The queue contains execution-ready beads that have not been attempted.
97	- The queue is blocked and operator action is required.
98	- The operator asks "why isn't anything running?" or a similar capacity question.
99	
100	The agent must not autonomously start `ddx work` from `queue_steward` mode.

input.description: Check GraphQL schema for audit event

data.output:
3368:  """Approve a proposed operator-prompt bead, transitioning it from proposed → open so the work can claim it. Requires a valid CSRF token. The originating identity must be on the per-project auto-approve allowlist; ts-net identities are never permitted (locked decision). An audit event of kind operator_prompt_approve records the approver. Errors when the bead is missing, is not an operator-prompt, is not in the proposed status, or the caller is not on the allowlist."""
3371:  """Cancel a proposed operator-prompt bead, transitioning it from proposed → cancelled so it never enters the work. Requires a valid CSRF token. Any trusted identity (localhost OR ts-net) may cancel a proposal — the allowlist gates auto-approval, not rejection. An audit event of kind operator_prompt_cancel records the canceller. Errors when the bead is missing, is not an operator-prompt, or is not in the proposed status."""
3374:  """Requeue an existing run by reopening its originating bead so the work can claim it again. Atomic with respect to the mandatory idempotencyKey: concurrent calls with the same key dedupe to the originating bead and never enqueue twice. An audit event of kind run_requeue is appended to the originating bead capturing operator identity, run id, and the optional layer override."""
3477:"""Result of operatorPromptApprove. The bead has transitioned from proposed → open and an audit event has been appended."""
3483:"""Result of operatorPromptCancel. The bead has transitioned from proposed → cancelled and an audit event has been appended."""
3495:  """Optional layer override (one of "work", "try", "run") recorded on the audit event for downstream handlers; ignored by the basic re-queue path."""

data.text: The spec content appears to be mostly in place. Let me check the FEAT-008 run-detail section more carefully.

data.output:
100	
101	### Run-detail page
102	
103	The shared run-detail page is the canonical deep surface for anything exposed
104	through the Runs tab.
105	
106	- Trust boundary: anyone with project read access can inspect the raw prompt,
107	  response, stderr, and tool-call arguments attached to visible `try` and
108	  `run` records. The page does not redact those fields inside the project
109	  boundary.
110	- Evidence browser whitelist: the Evidence tab renders only `*.txt`, `*.md`,
111	  `manifest.json`, `prompt.md`, and `result.json` inline, and only when the
112	  file is 64 KB or smaller. Other files remain download-only. The evidence
113	  browser does not render Markdown as HTML.
114	
115	- `layer=run` rows expand to AgentSession transcript / billing / cached
116	  tokens / stderr (the old Sessions detail).
117	- `layer=try` rows expand to the `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` bundle
118	  metadata, check results, and verdict (the old Executions detail).
119	- `layer=work` rows expand to queue inputs and child run links.
120	
121	**URL deprecation policy.** During the deprecation window, the legacy
122	URLs respond with **302 redirects (NOT 301)** to the matching filtered
123	Runs URL with all query params preserved, plus a `Sunset` response
124	header that names the cutoff date for the redirects:
125	
126	| Legacy URL | Redirect target |
127	|---|---|
128	| `/sessions` | `/runs?layer=run` |
129	| `/sessions#<id>` | `/runs?layer=run&session=<id>` |
130	| `/executions[?bead=&verdict=&harness=&q=&after=]` | `/runs?layer=try` with the original query string preserved verbatim |
131	| `/executions/[id]` | `/runs/[runId]?bundle=true` |
132	
133	302 (not 301) is intentional: bookmarks and external link aggregators
134	should not cache the redirect permanently while we still own the legacy
135	routes. The redirect response carries `Sunset: <RFC 9745 date>` and the
136	project nav drops the Sessions/Executions entries once the redirects and
137	the internal-link sweep land (`shellRoutes.ts`, `BeadDetail.svelte`,
138	commits page, agent page, Workers detail).
139	
140	### Build Pipeline
141	
142	```
143	cli/internal/server/frontend/   → SvelteKit + TypeScript
144	  bun run build                 → cli/internal/server/frontend/build/
145	cli/internal/server/            → Go with embed.FS
146	  go build                      → ddx-server (single binary with embedded UI)
147	```
148	
149	During development, SvelteKit's dev server proxies `/graphql` to the running Go server.
150	
151	### Technology Choices
152	
153	| Layer | Choice | Why |
154	|-------|--------|-----|
155	| Frontend framework | **Svelte 5** | Compile-time optimizations, no Virtual DOM |
156	| Build tool | **SvelteKit** | Router, layout, API routes, hooks, adapter-static for embedding |
157	| Styling | **Tailwind CSS** | Consistent with website (Hextra uses Tailwind) |
158	| Data fetching | **graphql-request + graphql-ws** | Lightweight GraphQL client with typed queries and WebSocket subscriptions |
159	| Graph visualization | **D3.js** or **Cytoscape.js** | For document dependency graph rendering |
160	| Embedding | **Go embed.FS** | Compile frontend into the binary — no external files needed |
161	| Go HTTP | **Chi** or **net/http** | Standard library compatible routing |
162	| Go GraphQL | **gqlgen** | Schema-first, code-generated resolvers |
163	
164	### Alternatives Considered
165	
166	**Templ + htmx** — simpler (no JS toolchain), but the dependency graph visualization and interactive bead board need more interactivity than htmx provides well. Templ + htmx is great for CRUD; we need a richer graph/dashboard experience.
167	
168	**Local-first with CRDT sync** — interesting for offline use but overkill for v1. The UI is primarily read-heavy (browsing docs, viewing graphs). Can be added later with PowerSync or ElectricSQL if needed.
169	
170	## Requirements
171	
172	### Functional
173	
174	1. **Artifact browser, viewer, editor, and regeneration**
175	   - List artifacts by category (prompts, personas, patterns, templates, ADRs,
176	     SDs, diagrams, wireframes, generated images, PDFs)
177	   - Full-text search across textual artifact content, sidecar metadata, and
178	     markdown frontmatter
179	   - View markdown artifacts rendered as markdown with syntax highlighting

data.text: Good - FEAT-008 already has the run-detail subsection. Let me check the FEAT-010 spec for the resolver contract and whether the audit event exists in the server.

data.output:
1380	
1381	The canonical deep loader for a run detail surface is `run(id:)`. It returns
1382	the run-layer fields used by FEAT-008:
1383	
1384	- `prompt`
1385	- `response`
1386	- `stderr`
1387	- `bundleFiles[]`
1388	
1389	The tool-call resolver is the `toolCalls(first, after)` stream exposed via
1390	`runToolCalls(id:, first:, after:)`. It returns the normalized tool-call
1391	stream as a paginated `RunToolCallConnection`, where each node projects the
1392	canonical drain-time `ToolCallEntry` shape:
1393	
1394	- `tool`
1395	- `input`
1396	- `output`
1397	- `duration_ms`
1398	- `error`
1399	
1400	`ToolCallEntry` is the normalized agent-side record of one tool execution. DDx
1401	persists the stream at drain time so the run substrate can page the stored
1402	sequence without rehydrating raw agent logs.
1403	
1404	The `bundleFile(path)` lookup is exposed via `runBundleFile(id:, path:)`. It
1405	is confined to the run's bundle root, rejects path traversal, absolute paths,
1406	and symlink escapes, and inlines content only for the whitelist of small text
1407	files documented by FEAT-008.
1408	
1409	### Layer-to-substrate mapping for the Runs UI
1410	
1411	The web Runs view (FEAT-008 §5, FEAT-021) renders three layer chips
1412	backed by the unified substrate plus the legacy detail backings. The
1413	shared run-detail tab strip is the canonical drill-down surface used by
1414	FEAT-008, FEAT-019, and FEAT-021:
1415	
1416	| Chip | Substrate row | Detail backing for row expansion |
1417	|---|---|---|
1418	| `work` | `layer: 3` Run record | Layer-3 record's queue inputs / stop-condition log / child layer-2 attempt ids; the shared tab strip exposes `overview` only |
1419	| `try` | `layer: 2` Run record | The `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` bundle attached to the layer-2 record (manifest, prompt, result, checks, verdict); the shared tab strip exposes `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `tools`, and `evidence` |
1420	| `run` | `layer: 1` Run record | The associated `AgentSession` row (prompt / response / stderr / billing / cached-token detail) joined onto the layer-1 record; the shared tab strip exposes `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `session`, `tools`, and `evidence` |
1421	
1422	`AgentSession` rows that have no parent layer-2 attempt (raw `ddx
1423	agent log` invocations) surface as synthesized `layer=run` Runs rows
1424	keyed by session id, so no agent-session row is dropped during the
1425	Sessions/Executions tab retirement.
1426	
1427	The shared tab strip is:
1428	
1429	- `work` → `overview`
1430	- `try` → `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `tools`, `evidence`
1431	- `run` → `overview`, `prompt`, `response`, `session`, `tools`, `evidence`
1432	
1433	## User Stories
1434	
1435	### US-090: Developer Inspects a Layer-1 Run
1436	
1437	**As a** developer running an ad-hoc agent invocation
1438	**I want** to inspect the resulting run record
1439	**So that** I can audit prompt, response, tokens, and side-effects
